🦁
Africa

Lion

Panthera leo
Status Vulnerable
Habitat Savannah, Grasslands, Open Woodlands
Diet Carnivore
Lifespan 10-14 years (wild)
Weight 120-190 kg (male), 90-130 kg (female)
×

The lion (Panthera leo) reigns as one of Africa's most iconic predators, embodying strength, majesty, and the raw power of the wild. As the only truly social cat, lions live in prides that showcase complex cooperative behaviors rarely seen among felines. These magnificent hunters, with males weighing up to 190 kg, dominate the savannas and grasslands of sub-Saharan Africa. Their distinctive roars, audible from 8 kilometers away, and their regal bearing have earned them the title 'King of Beasts' across human cultures for millennia.

Habitat & Range

Lions inhabit the savannas, grasslands, and open woodlands of sub-Saharan Africa, with the strongest populations found in East Africa and Southern Africa. They require large territories with sufficient prey density—typically 8-100 square kilometers depending on prey availability. Key strongholds include the Serengeti-Mara ecosystem, Kruger National Park, and the Okavango Delta. Lions avoid dense rainforest and desert interiors but thrive in edge habitats where woodland meets open plains.

Behavior & Social Structure

Lions are unique among cats for their social structure. Prides typically consist of 2-3 related adult males, 5-10 related females, and their cubs. Females remain in their birth pride for life, while males leave at 2-3 years to form coalitions and eventually take over new prides. Cooperative hunting allows lions to bring down large prey like buffalo and giraffe. Complex social bonds include communal nursing, synchronized estrus, and group territorial defense marked by roaring and scent-marking.

Diet & Nutrition

As obligate carnivores, lions primarily hunt large ungulates including wildebeest, zebra, buffalo, and various antelope species. A single lion can consume up to 15 kg of meat in one feeding, though they average 5-7 kg daily. Females do most hunting, using cooperative strategies to surround and ambush prey. Lions also scavenge when opportunity arises, often stealing kills from hyenas and other predators. Their presence helps regulate prey populations and maintain ecosystem health.

Key Facts

  • Only truly social cat, living in prides of up to 30 individuals
  • Male's roar can be heard up to 8 kilometers away
  • Females do 85-90% of the hunting for the pride
  • Can run at speeds up to 80 km/h in short bursts
  • Sleep 16-20 hours per day, most active at dawn and dusk
  • Critical apex predator maintaining ecosystem balance

Conservation Status

Classified as Vulnerable by the IUCN Red List. African lion populations have declined by approximately 43% over the past two decades, with fewer than 25,000 individuals remaining in the wild. Primary threats include human-wildlife conflict, habitat loss and fragmentation, prey depletion due to poaching, and retaliatory killings by livestock owners. Conservation efforts focus on community-based programs, anti-poaching initiatives, and creating wildlife corridors to connect fragmented habitats.

Primary Threats

  • Human-wildlife conflict and retaliatory killings
  • Habitat loss and fragmentation from agriculture
  • Prey depletion due to illegal bushmeat poaching
  • Trophy hunting in some range countries
  • Disease transmission from domestic animals

Related Species

🐆 Cheetah 🐘 African Elephant

Frequently Asked Questions

How big is an Lion?

Lions typically weigh 120-190 kg (male), 90-130 kg (female) and can be identified by their distinctive features. They are native to Savannah, Grasslands, Open Woodlands.

Are Lions endangered?

The Lion is currently classified as Vulnerable by the IUCN Red List. Classified as Vulnerable by the IUCN Red List. African lion populations have declined by approximately 43% over the past two decades, with fewer than ...

What do Lions eat?

Lions are carnivore. As obligate carnivores, lions primarily hunt large ungulates including wildebeest, zebra, buffalo, and various antelope ...

How long do Lions live?

In the wild, Lions typically live for 10-14 years (wild). Their lifespan can vary based on environmental conditions and conservation efforts.
